h1Title: Bell's Palsy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Bell's palsy is a sudden, unilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy caused by inflammation and swelling of the seventh cranial nerve, producing weakness or complete paralysis of all muscles on one side of the face. Roughly 40,000 Americans develop Bell's palsy each year, giving a lifetime risk of approximately 1 in 60. Onset is typically within hours: patients wake unable to close one eye, smile symmetrically, or move one eyebrow. Untreated, about 70% of patients recover fully within six months, but 15–29% are left with lasting weakness, synkinesis, or contracture. High-dose oral corticosteroids started within 72 hours of onset improve the chance of complete recovery to roughly 85–90% (NNT ≈ 8). Antivirals add modest benefit in severe cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 11–40 per 100,000 population per year; lifetime cumulative risk approximately 1.7% (Sullivan et al., Lancet 2007)
keyStats.demographics: Equal sex distribution; peak incidence ages 15–45; pregnant women and people with diabetes have 3–4× higher risk
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset in the third and fourth decade; can occur at any age including childhood
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 300,000–500,000 new cases worldwide annually; most common cause of acute unilateral facial palsy

definition: Bell's palsy (ICD-10: G51.0) is an acute, idiopathic, unilateral lower motor neuron facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) palsy caused by inflammation of the nerve within the bony facial canal of the petrous temporal bone. It accounts for 60–75% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy. The precise etiology remains under investigation, but reactivation of latent herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) in the geniculate ganglion is the leading hypothesis, supported by detection of HSV-1 DNA in endoneurial fluid at surgery and in mouse models replicating the condition. Inflammation produces oedema that compresses the nerve within its narrow bony canal, causing demyelination or, in severe cases, axonal degeneration. The entire ipsilateral face is affected — forehead, eye, and lower face — which distinguishes peripheral palsy from central (upper motor neuron) facial weakness where the forehead is spared because of bilateral cortical representation. Bell's palsy is diagnosed clinically after exclusion of identifiable causes such as Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus), Lyme disease, otitis media, parotid tumours, and sarcoidosis. Management is led by neurologists, otolaryngologists, or general physicians, with ophthalmologic input when corneal exposure is significant.

typesClassification.description: Normal or near-normal function with minimal visible asymmetry. Complete eye closure with minimal effort. Recovery rate approaches 100% without treatment.
Noticeable weakness with incomplete eye closure on effort. Distinguishable asymmetry but not disfiguring. Full recovery with early steroid therapy in roughly 85–90% of cases.
No movement or only minimal movement detectable. Lagophthalmos with corneal exposure risk. Permanent sequelae — synkinesis, contracture, crocodile tears — more likely. Carries the worst prognosis.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of unilateral facial weakness or complete paralysis, usually developing over 24–72 hours, affecting both the upper (forehead, eyebrow) and lower (cheek, mouth) face on the affected side.
Inability to close the eye fully (lagophthalmos) on the affected side, causing dryness, tearing, or redness due to corneal exposure — a direct risk to vision.
Drooping of the corner of the mouth with deviation of the mouth toward the healthy side when smiling or speaking, causing drooling and difficulty eating.
Loss of the nasolabial fold and inability to raise the eyebrow or wrinkle the forehead on the affected side, distinguishing peripheral from central facial palsy.
Altered taste (dysgeusia) or loss of taste on the anterior two-thirds of the tongue, reflecting involvement of the chorda tympani branch of cranial nerve VII.
Hyperacusis — abnormal sensitivity to loud sounds — from paralysis of the stapedius muscle that normally dampens ossicular vibration.
Post-auricular or mastoid pain preceding or accompanying the facial weakness in approximately 60% of patients, sometimes radiating into the ipsilateral ear or neck.
Decreased salivation and tearing on the affected side from disruption of secretomotor fibres running with cranial nerve VII.
Slurred or muffled speech because of difficulty controlling lip movements and oral seal on the affected side.
In resolution, involuntary co-contraction (synkinesis) where eye closure triggers corner-of-mouth movement or vice versa, from aberrant regeneration of nerve fibres.
earlyWarningSigns: New post-auricular or mastoid aching pain on one side that develops before any visible facial weakness — occurs in roughly 60% of cases and is sometimes the first symptom by 24 hours.
Subtle asymmetry when smiling, difficulty whistling, or a sense of heaviness or numbness over one cheek before frank weakness is apparent.
Increasing dryness or grittiness in one eye, which may reflect early impairment of orbicularis oculi before the patient notices visible weakness.
A sudden change in taste or metallic sensation on one side of the tongue accompanying a new ear or jaw ache.
emergencySigns: New facial weakness with any limb weakness, dysarthria, dysphagia, or diplopia — this combination suggests a central lesion such as stroke or brainstem event and warrants emergency brain imaging.

Facial palsy with vesicular eruption in the ear canal, concha, or soft palate — Ramsay Hunt syndrome requires urgent antiviral therapy (acyclovir 800 mg five times daily) to prevent permanent paralysis and sensorineural hearing loss.
Rapidly progressive bilateral facial weakness — consider Guillain-Barré syndrome, which may cause bilateral facial palsies and requires neurological emergency assessment.
Signs of corneal ulceration: a red, painful eye with cloudy cornea in a patient unable to close the eye — requires same-day ophthalmology review to prevent irreversible visual loss.
Facial palsy accompanied by headache, fever, neck stiffness, or altered consciousness — raise suspicion for bacterial meningitis or other CNS infection requiring immediate evaluation.

causes.description: The dominant hypothesis. HSV-1 DNA has been detected in endoneurial fluid from the facial canal in patients with Bell's palsy at higher rates than controls. The virus is thought to reactivate in the geniculate ganglion during states of physiological stress, causing demyelinating inflammation within the bony canal. Mouse models of intracranial HSV-1 infection reproduce the palsy.

causes.cause: Facial nerve oedema within the bony facial canal
causes.description: Regardless of the trigger, the common final pathway is inflammatory swelling of the nerve in the narrow facial canal (approximately 2 mm diameter). Pressure within this inelastic channel reduces blood flow, causing ischaemia, demyelination, and in severe cases axonal degeneration. Severity correlates with the degree of nerve swelling detected on gadolinium-enhanced MRI.

An autoimmune component is supported by the histopathologic finding of lymphocytic infiltration around the nerve and evidence of T-cell-mediated demyelination, analogous to other cranial nerve inflammations. Some cases follow upper respiratory tract infections, suggesting molecular mimicry or bystander activation.

causes.cause: Herpes zoster (Ramsay Hunt syndrome) — a distinct identifiable cause
causes.description: Varicella-zoster virus reactivation in the geniculate ganglion causes a more severe facial palsy combined with otic vesicles, otalgia, and sensorineural hearing loss. Ramsay Hunt syndrome is responsible for 12% of cases initially labelled Bell's palsy. It carries a worse prognosis — full recovery in fewer than 50% versus approximately 70% for true idiopathic Bell's palsy.

Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi), sarcoidosis, otitis media, parotid neoplasms, cholesteatoma, and trauma to the temporal bone all produce peripheral facial palsy through different mechanisms. These are not true Bell's palsy but must be excluded before an idiopathic label is applied.

Bell's palsy occurs 3–4 times more frequently in pregnant women than in age-matched non-pregnant women, with the highest risk in the third trimester and the immediate postpartum period. The mechanism is incompletely understood but may relate to altered fluid retention increasing nerve canal pressure and immune modulation in pregnancy.

riskFactors.description: Risk 3–4× higher in pregnant women, peaking in the third trimester and first week postpartum. Oedematous fluid retention in the facial canal during pregnancy may compress the nerve.

People with type 2 diabetes have approximately 2.9× higher risk of Bell's palsy. Diabetic microvascular disease affecting the vasa nervorum may predispose the facial nerve to ischaemia under inflammatory stress.

Impaired viral latency control increases the risk of HSV-1 and VZV reactivation. HIV-positive individuals have higher rates of facial palsy, both from direct infection and from immune reconstitution syndromes.
A preceding viral URTI in the weeks before palsy onset is reported by 30–60% of patients, suggesting viral infection or post-infectious immune activation as a precipitant.
Approximately 8–14% of Bell's palsy cases have a first-degree family member with the condition, suggesting a heritable anatomical narrow facial canal or immune susceptibility.
Elevated blood pressure is associated with a modest but consistent increase in Bell's palsy incidence, possibly through microvascular effects on nerve blood supply.

Physical and psychological stress are well-established precipitants of HSV-1 reactivation, which is the putative trigger for Bell's palsy. However, causal data in humans is epidemiologically difficult to isolate.

affectedDemographics: Equal prevalence in men and women across the working-age population; some studies note a slight male preponderance after age 40.
Peak incidence in the 15–45 age group; a second peak occurs after age 60 in some population-based studies.
Pregnant women experience 3–4× higher incidence, especially in the third trimester (Gillman et al., J Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2002).
People with diabetes mellitus have a relative risk of approximately 2.9 compared with normoglycaemic populations (Adour et al., Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2002).
Geographically universal — Bell's palsy occurs in all populations worldwide without significant ethnic clustering, unlike some autoimmune neuropathies.
diagnosisOverview: Bell's palsy is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion. The hallmark is sudden onset of unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness affecting the forehead — differentiating it from a central (cortical or subcortical) lesion, which spares the forehead because of bilateral cortical representation. The House-Brackmann grading scale (I to VI) classifies severity and predicts prognosis. Initial assessment focuses on excluding identifiable causes: examine the ear for vesicles (Ramsay Hunt), palpate the parotid for masses, assess for other cranial nerve deficits, check for Lyme-endemic exposure, and evaluate for systemic diseases including sarcoidosis and HIV. Routine blood work in uncomplicated cases should include fasting glucose and HbA1c to detect underlying diabetes. Serological testing for Lyme disease (ELISA, Western blot) is indicated in endemic areas or where exposure is suspected. Electrodiagnostic studies — electroneuronography (ENoG) and needle electromyography — are reserved for severe Grade V–VI palsy after 14 days when the prognosis remains unclear; they help distinguish neurapraxia (favourable) from wallerian degeneration (guarded). Gadolinium-enhanced MRI of the brain and facial nerve demonstrates nerve enhancement in the labyrinthine segment and geniculate ganglion in most Bell's palsy cases, but is not required for diagnosis. It should be obtained when palsy fails to improve by three months, when other neurological findings are present, or when a central lesion must be excluded.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Grades facial palsy severity from I (normal) to VI (complete paralysis) and documents forehead involvement to confirm peripheral versus central pattern
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The examiner asks the patient to raise eyebrows, close eyes tightly, smile, and show teeth. Each movement is graded. Takes 5 minutes. Repeated at follow-up visits to track recovery.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for undiagnosed diabetes mellitus, a major risk factor for Bell's palsy and a predictor of slower recovery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw at the first clinic visit. Results return within 24 hours. Fasting for 8 hours beforehand is ideal for glucose; HbA1c does not require fasting.

diagnosticTests.test: Lyme serology (ELISA and reflex Western blot)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes Lyme neuroborreliosis as a cause of facial palsy, especially in endemic regions (northeastern US, parts of Europe)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw. ELISA result in 1–3 days; Western blot confirmation adds 3–5 days. A positive result changes management to antibiotics rather than antivirals.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies degeneration in severe palsy after 14 days to guide prognosis and surgical decompression decisions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ENoG applies electrical stimulation over the stylomastoid foramen and records compound action potential amplitude. EMG uses a fine needle in the facial muscles. The procedure takes 30–45 minutes and may be mildly uncomfortable.

diagnosticTests.test: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI brain and temporal bones
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects nerve enhancement, rules out central lesions, parotid tumours, or cholesteatoma in atypical or slow-to-recover cases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30–45 minutes in the scanner with an intravenous contrast injection. Standard brain and dedicated temporal bone sequences. Ordered when the clinical picture is atypical or recovery is absent at three months.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for associated sensorineural hearing loss or conductive loss suggesting Ramsay Hunt syndrome or otitis media
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient wears headphones and presses a button when tones are heard. Tympanometry involves a brief ear probe. Takes 15–20 minutes. Often performed by audiology on the same clinic visit.

treatmentOverview: First-line treatment is high-dose oral prednisolone, which improves the rate of complete recovery by approximately 15 percentage points over placebo. NICE guideline NG170 (2019) and the American Academy of Neurology (AAN) practice parameter (2012) both recommend prednisolone 50–60 mg per day for 10 days started within 72 hours of symptom onset. This is the most important intervention. The number needed to treat for one additional full recovery is approximately 8. Antivirals — acyclovir 400 mg five times daily or valacyclovir 1,000 mg three times daily for 7 days — as monotherapy show no benefit over placebo. In combination with corticosteroids, pooled meta-analyses suggest a small additional benefit in severe palsy (Grade V–VI), and the ENGEL and Cochrane reviews support adding antivirals in severe cases, though the evidence is less robust than for steroids. Treatment should begin within 72 hours of symptom onset — the window in which steroids achieve full benefit. After 72 hours, benefit diminishes; treatment is generally not started after 7 days. Eye care is critical from day one: artificial tear drops every 1–2 hours while awake, lubricating eye ointment at night, moisture-chamber spectacles or an eye patch to prevent corneal abrasion, and ophthalmology referral when lagophthalmos is significant. Facial physiotherapy, electrical stimulation, and acupuncture have insufficient trial evidence to be recommended routinely. Surgical facial nerve decompression (transmastoid or middle fossa approach) remains controversial — a 2011 Cochrane review found insufficient evidence and, given that most patients recover, the risks of surgery (sensorineural hearing loss 1–3%) generally outweigh benefits except in selected severe cases with documented degeneration on ENoG.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisolone (50 mg daily for 10 days, or 60 mg/day tapered over 10–14 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line therapy. Reduces nerve oedema and demyelinating inflammation within the facial canal. Must be started within 72 hours of symptom onset for full effect. NICE NG170 and AAN 2012 guidelines endorse this as the treatment of choice.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Absolute increase in complete recovery rate approximately 12–15 percentage points vs. placebo; NNT ≈ 8 (Sullivan et al., Lancet 2007; Engström et al., Lancet 2008)

medicalTreatments.name: Valacyclovir (1,000 mg three times daily for 7 days) or acyclovir (400 mg five times daily for 7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Added to prednisolone in severe Grade IV–VI palsy. Suppresses HSV-1 reactivation and targets the probable viral trigger. Monotherapy has not shown benefit over placebo in RCTs. Combined with steroids, some benefit in severe cases is suggested.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination therapy produces complete recovery in approximately 90% of severe cases vs. 77% with prednisolone alone in the Engström RCT (Lancet 2008, PMID 18571761)

medicalTreatments.name: Lubricating eye drops (carboxymethylcellulose 0.5%) and ocular lubricating ointment (paraffin-based)
medicalTreatments.description: Prevents corneal desiccation and ulceration due to lagophthalmos. Drops every 1–2 hours while awake; ointment before sleep. May require taping the eye shut at night or a moisture-chamber goggle during the day.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard of care for all grades of Bell's palsy with any degree of lagophthalmos; corneal injury prevented in >95% of patients with consistent use

medicalTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin type A (onabotulinum or abobotulinum) for synkinesis
medicalTreatments.description: Injected in small doses (1.25–2.5 U per injection point) into facial muscles affected by aberrant co-contractions (synkinesis) during recovery. Reduces involuntary movements that emerge as nerve fibres regenerate incorrectly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically meaningful reduction in synkinesis severity in 70–80% of injected patients; effects last 3–4 months, requiring repeat treatments (Bhidayasiri and Cardoso, Tremor Other Hyperkinet Mov 2018)

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and neuromuscular retraining
medicalTreatments.description: Biofeedback-based facial physiotherapy and mirror exercises help patients learn to isolate facial movements and reduce synkinesis during recovery. Most useful starting at 3–6 weeks when nerve recovery begins.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational studies and one small RCT report faster symmetry recovery and reduced synkinesis severity; insufficient evidence to quantify NNT

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical gold-weight implant to upper eyelid
medicalTreatments.description: A small gold or platinum weight (0.8–1.6 g) surgically inserted in the upper eyelid to aid passive eye closure when lagophthalmos persists after 6 months. Prevents corneal complications in permanent paralysis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Corneal protection achieved in approximately 90% of patients; fully reversible if nerve recovery occurs later

surgicalOptions.name: Facial nerve decompression (transmastoid or middle fossa approach)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical decompression of the facial canal to relieve external compression on the swollen nerve. Indicated only in severe Grade V–VI palsy with >90% degeneration on ENoG within 14 days. Risks include permanent sensorineural hearing loss (1–3%).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Insufficient high-quality RCT evidence; observational series suggest possible benefit in selected severe cases; most guidelines do not routinely recommend

surgicalOptions.name: Upper eyelid gold- or platinum-weight implant
surgicalOptions.description: Platinum chain (0.8–1.6 g) embedded in the upper eyelid to restore passive blink and protect the cornea in persistent lagophthalmos beyond 6 months.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Corneal protection in approximately 90% of patients; outcomes in published series with 3-year follow-up are consistently favourable

surgicalOptions.name: Static and dynamic facial reanimation
surgicalOptions.description: For permanent paralysis: static techniques (fascial slings, brow lifts, lower-lid tightening) restore symmetry at rest; dynamic techniques (cross-facial nerve graft, masseter-to-facial nerve transfer) restore volitional movement. Decisions made in specialist facial reanimation centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Nerve-to-nerve grafts produce grade III–IV House-Brackmann outcomes in 70–80% of patients when performed within 12–18 months of injury

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2010 Cochrane review found no significant difference in recovery rates between acupuncture and corticosteroids or sham acupuncture in four small trials. Not recommended as primary treatment but may offer subjective benefit when combined with conventional care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin B12 (methylcobalamin 1,500 µg daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: One small RCT (Jalaludin 1995, PMID 7550378) suggested faster recovery with methylcobalamin versus no treatment. Insufficient evidence for routine recommendation but carries negligible risk and is prescribed as adjunctive therapy in some Asian centres.

whySeeSpecialist: Neurologist or otolaryngologist assessment is advisable for all cases to confirm peripheral rather than central palsy, grade severity, and initiate early treatment. Referral to ophthalmology is urgent when the eye cannot fully close or corneal changes are detected.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a neurologist or ENT specialist with experience in cranial neuropathies for diagnosis and follow-up. For eye protection, an ophthalmologist skilled in corneal management is essential. Patients with synkinesis or incomplete recovery benefit from a facial physiotherapist or a plastic surgeon with facial reanimation experience.
hospitalCriteria: Availability of gadolinium-enhanced MRI and temporal bone imaging for atypical or non-recovering cases
ENT or neurology outpatient service for House-Brackmann grading and electrodiagnostic testing
Ophthalmology unit for corneal assessment and management of lagophthalmos
Facial physiotherapy service with neuromuscular retraining experience
Facial reanimation surgery capability for permanent paralysis cases
keyFacilities: Neurology and ENT outpatient clinics for initial diagnosis and monitoring
Ophthalmology services for eye protection management
Specialist facial paralysis rehabilitation centres for reanimation surgery and physiotherapy
preventionStrategies: Ensure complete vaccination against varicella-zoster virus to reduce the risk of herpes zoster reactivation (Ramsay Hunt syndrome), which causes a severe form of facial palsy.
Optimise blood glucose control in patients with diabetes, as diabetic Bell's palsy is more severe and recovers more slowly.
Manage hypertension with antihypertensive therapy to reduce facial nerve microvascular ischaemia risk.
Start antiviral suppressive therapy (acyclovir 400 mg twice daily) in immunocompromised patients with frequent HSV-1 reactivations to reduce future episodes.
lifestyleModifications: Apply lubricating eye drops every 1–2 hours while awake and ointment at night until the eye closes completely.
Tape the eyelid shut before sleeping or use moisture-chamber spectacles during the day to prevent corneal drying.
Eat soft, easily-chewed foods and chew on the unaffected side to reduce food trapping and aspiration risk.
Perform prescribed facial exercises or mirror feedback training once the nerve begins recovering, usually at 4–8 weeks.
Avoid wearing contact lenses on the affected eye until complete eye closure is restored.
Report any worsening weakness after initial improvement, new vesicles in the ear, hearing changes, or pain to the treating clinician promptly.

recommended: Soft, easily chewed foods (yogurt, mashed vegetables, porridge) to manage difficulty eating during acute palsy
High-protein foods (eggs, fish, lentils) to support nerve recovery and general health
Adequate fluid intake to prevent dehydration related to drooling and reduced oral intake
Foods rich in B vitamins (fortified cereals, meat, dairy, leafy greens) to support peripheral nerve health
avoid: Hard, crunchy foods that require bilateral chewing when oral muscle control is impaired
Hot beverages or soups without caution — reduced oral sensation can cause burns
Alcohol during the acute treatment phase with corticosteroids (gastric irritation risk)
Excessive caffeine if sleep is already disrupted by pain or eye discomfort

exerciseGuidelines: General aerobic exercise (walking, cycling) is safe and encouraged during recovery. Facial exercises — eyebrow raises, smiling, eye closure — should be started gently once nerve regeneration begins, typically 4–8 weeks into the illness, guided by a physiotherapist to minimise synkinesis.
dailyManagement: Apply lubricating eye drops on schedule (every 1–2 hours) and ointment each night without fail until full eye closure is restored.
Inspect the eye every morning for redness, cloudiness, or discharge signalling corneal damage — seek same-day ophthalmology care if any sign appears.
Take the full course of prescribed prednisolone with food; do not stop early even if swallowing is difficult.
Record daily a simple assessment of eye closure, smile symmetry, and forehead movement to track recovery and share at follow-up appointments.
Attend follow-up at 4 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months, or sooner if no improvement or new symptoms appear.
Notify the treating clinician if a vesicular rash appears in or around the ear — this changes the diagnosis to Ramsay Hunt syndrome requiring urgent antiviral dose adjustment.
prognosis: The overall prognosis for Bell's palsy is favourable: approximately 70% of patients achieve complete, unsupported spontaneous recovery within 3–6 months. With early high-dose corticosteroids started within 72 hours, the complete-recovery rate rises to approximately 85–90%. Recovery is most likely in those with Grade I–III initial palsy, where near-100% full recovery is expected. In Grade V–VI complete paralysis, only 50–60% achieve full recovery without steroids; with steroids, 71–76% recover completely (Sullivan et al., Lancet 2007). Persistent residua occur in 15–29% of patients and include partial weakness, synkinesis (involuntary facial co-movements), and crocodile tears (gustatory epiphora). These do not improve further after 12 months and require management with botulinum toxin or physiotherapy. Poor prognostic markers are: complete paralysis at onset, age over 60, hypertension, and absent electrical activity on ENoG at 14 days. Recurrent Bell's palsy occurs in 7–10% of patients, more commonly ipsilateral than contralateral, and should prompt investigation for underlying conditions such as sarcoidosis or Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome.
recoveryTimeline: Mild cases (Grade I–II): significant improvement within 3–4 weeks, full recovery by 2–3 months. Moderate cases (Grade III–IV): improvement beginning 3–6 weeks after onset, full or near-full recovery typically by 3–6 months. Severe cases (Grade V–VI): initial signs of regeneration at 3–6 months; final functional outcome not assessable before 12 months. Synkinesis develops in 15–25% of severe cases as regeneration progresses, usually appearing at 3–6 months.
complications: Corneal ulceration or permanent corneal scarring from lagophthalmos and exposure keratopathy — prevented by consistent eye lubrication and prompts ophthalmology review.
Synkinesis (involuntary co-contractions): eye closure triggers corner-of-mouth movement and vice versa, due to aberrant nerve fibre regeneration; affects roughly 15–25% of severe cases.
Crocodile tears (Bogorad syndrome): inappropriate lacrimation during eating from aberrant reinnervation of the lacrimal gland by fibres originally destined for salivary glands.
Hemifacial contracture: permanent shortening of facial muscles on the affected side after severe axonal degeneration, narrowing the palpebral fissure and drawing the mouth upward.

Psychological impact: depression, social anxiety, and reduced quality of life are documented in 28–49% of patients with moderate-to-severe palsy; referral to psychological support services should be routine.
Permanent facial asymmetry affecting appearance, speech, and eating — requiring facial reanimation surgery in patients with persistent paralysis beyond 12–18 months.

supportResources.name: Facial Palsy UK
supportResources.description: UK charity offering patient guides, physiotherapy directories, and peer support for all types of facial palsy including Bell's palsy.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Otolaryngology — Bell's Palsy Patient Guide
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information from the professional body for ear, nose, and throat specialists in North America.

supportResources.name: NICE Clinical Knowledge Summary — Bell's Palsy
supportResources.description: Practical UK primary-care guidance on diagnosis, treatment thresholds, and follow-up for Bell's palsy.

supportResources.name: Cochrane Reviews — Bell's Palsy
supportResources.description: Open-access systematic reviews of corticosteroid and antiviral treatment evidence in Bell's palsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Stroke-related central facial palsy spares the forehead because both cerebral hemispheres contribute to forehead representation; in Bell's palsy the entire face including the forehead is paralysed on one side. Stroke also produces limb weakness, speech disturbance, or visual changes, which are absent in Bell's palsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acoustic Neuroma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) causes progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss and tinnitus before facial weakness develops. Bell's palsy does not produce hearing loss unless caused by Ramsay Hunt syndrome. MRI clearly distinguishes a mass lesion from idiopathic palsy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: MS can produce a facial palsy through a demyelinating plaque in the facial nucleus or fascicles within the pons. MS palsy may be central (forehead-sparing) or peripheral in pattern, and occurs alongside other neurological symptoms and MRI white-matter lesions. Bell's palsy is a single isolated event with no brain parenchymal disease on MRI.

confusedWithConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Trigeminal neuralgia (cranial nerve V) causes severe stabbing pain over the face but produces no facial muscle weakness or paralysis — the sensory and motor distributions are distinct. Bell's palsy weakens facial muscles but typically causes only mild sensory changes and does not produce the excruciating paroxysmal pain of trigeminal neuralgia.

relatedConditions.name: Trigeminal Neuralgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both affect the cranial nerves of the face; trigeminal neuralgia involves nerve V (pain), Bell's palsy involves nerve VII (movement). Occasionally co-occur in sarcoidosis or skull base disease.

relatedConditions.name: Acoustic Neuroma
relatedConditions.relevance: Tumour at the cerebellopontine angle that can compress cranial nerves VII and VIII, mimicking Bell's palsy with progressive rather than acute onset.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple Sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Demyelinating plaques in the pons or brainstem can produce facial weakness that enters the differential diagnosis for Bell's palsy, especially in younger patients.

relatedConditions.name: Clonic Hemifacial Spasm
relatedConditions.relevance: Involuntary unilateral facial contractions caused by vascular compression of the facial nerve root; may occur as a sequela of Bell's palsy from aberrant nerve regeneration.

relatedConditions.name: Atypical Facial Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent facial pain that may follow Bell's palsy, particularly post-herpetic neuralgia after Ramsay Hunt syndrome.

faqs.question: What is Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: Bell's palsy is sudden paralysis or weakness of the muscles on one side of the face caused by inflammation of the seventh cranial nerve. It typically develops over hours, affects forehead and lower face equally, and is not caused by a stroke. Most patients recover fully within six months, especially with early steroid treatment.

faqs.question: How do I know if it's Bell's palsy or a stroke?
faqs.answer: Bell's palsy involves the entire face including the forehead. A stroke usually spares the forehead because of bilateral brain control of forehead muscles. Stroke also causes limb weakness, speech problems, or vision changes absent in Bell's palsy. Any doubt warrants immediate emergency assessment.

faqs.question: What causes Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: The most likely cause is reactivation of herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) in the geniculate ganglion, causing the facial nerve to swell inside its narrow bony canal. Inflammation compresses the nerve, producing weakness. Other triggers include herpes zoster (Ramsay Hunt), Lyme disease, and pregnancy, but in many cases no cause is found.

faqs.question: Does Bell's palsy go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Approximately 70% of patients recover fully without treatment within six months. With oral prednisolone started within 72 hours of onset, about 85–90% achieve complete recovery. Without steroids, roughly 30% are left with some permanent weakness or synkinesis. Starting treatment promptly is the single most important factor.

faqs.question: How long does Bell's palsy last?
faqs.answer: Mild to moderate cases typically improve within 3–6 weeks and recover fully by 3 months. Severe complete paralysis can take 6–12 months for maximum recovery, and some permanent residua remain at 12 months. Recovery after 12 months is uncommon and suggests a different underlying cause.

faqs.question: Is Bell's palsy treated with steroids?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral prednisolone 50–60 mg daily for 10 days started within 72 hours is the recommended first-line treatment per NICE NG170 and AAN guidelines. This doubles the chance of achieving complete recovery compared with watchful waiting. Steroids are most effective the earlier they are taken after symptom onset.

faqs.question: Should I add antivirals to steroids for Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: Guidelines recommend adding valacyclovir or acyclovir to prednisolone in severe Bell's palsy (Grade IV–VI). For mild to moderate cases, steroids alone are sufficient. Antivirals alone are not effective and should not replace steroids. The combination produces complete recovery in approximately 90% of severe cases.

faqs.question: How do I protect my eye in Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: Apply lubricating eye drops every 1–2 hours while awake and a thick lubricating ointment at bedtime. Tape the eye shut at night or use a moisture-chamber goggle. Wear protective eyewear outdoors. See an ophthalmologist if the eye becomes red or painful — corneal damage can develop quickly when the eye cannot close.

faqs.question: Is Bell's palsy painful?
faqs.answer: About 60% of patients experience pain behind the ear or over the mastoid in the 24–48 hours before or at onset of weakness. The pain usually resolves within a week. If severe ear pain develops alongside facial palsy and a blistering rash in the ear canal, this suggests Ramsay Hunt syndrome, which requires urgent antiviral treatment.

faqs.question: Can Bell's palsy cause permanent damage?
faqs.answer: Permanent damage occurs in 15–29% of patients. Residua include partial facial weakness, synkinesis (eye closing triggers lip movement), crocodile tears, and facial contracture. These are more common after complete paralysis at onset or delayed treatment. Botulinum toxin injections and physiotherapy manage synkinesis effectively.

faqs.question: Can Bell's palsy come back?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in approximately 7–10% of Bell's palsy patients, most often on the same side. Recurrent episodes should prompt evaluation for sarcoidosis, Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, or other underlying conditions that predispose to cranial nerve inflammation.

faqs.question: Does Bell's palsy affect hearing?
faqs.answer: Bell's palsy itself does not cause hearing loss, but it can produce hyperacusis — sensitivity to loud sounds — from paralysis of the stapedius muscle. If hearing loss occurs alongside facial palsy, Ramsay Hunt syndrome (herpes zoster oticus) must be suspected, as it affects both cranial nerves VII and VIII.

faqs.question: Can children get Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bell's palsy occurs at any age including childhood and infancy. Children generally have an excellent prognosis with full recovery in over 90% of cases. The diagnosis requires the same exclusion of central lesions as in adults. Steroid dosing is adjusted for weight in children.

faqs.question: Is Bell's palsy more common in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pregnant women develop Bell's palsy at 3–4 times the rate of non-pregnant women, with highest risk in the third trimester and early postpartum period. Oral prednisolone at standard doses is safe in pregnancy. The prognosis is similar to non-pregnant patients when treatment is started promptly.

faqs.question: What is synkinesis after Bell's palsy?
faqs.answer: Synkinesis is involuntary co-movement of facial muscles during Bell's palsy recovery, caused by nerve fibres re-growing along incorrect pathways. Examples include eye closure triggering corner-of-mouth movement, or smiling causing the eye to narrow. Botulinum toxin injections and neuromuscular physiotherapy are the main treatments.
